topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a poorly aimed projectile. It was a formal notification from OmniCorp, detailing a recent internal restructuring. My friend, Mark, had casually mentioned he was in a high-level project management role at OmniCorp for the last six months. I clicked the attachment, and the reality slammed into me. The title listed was 'Junior Data Entry Specialist.' A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, and I gripped my mouse so tightly my knuckles turned white. I stared at the screen, the words blurring, before I slowly, deliberately, minimized the window. When Mark finally called, his voice sounded annoyingly breezy. "So, how's the big promotion going?" I took a slow, measured breath, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face. "Oh, great, Mark. Just... busy." The air in the room suddenly felt too thin.

[story 2]

I found the discrepancy tucked away in a shared cloud drive—a meticulously organized folder of Mark’s 'professional' documents. The official LinkedIn profile was pristine, showcasing impressive achievements. But buried beneath a folder labeled 'Personal Archives,' there were screenshots of job applications he’d discarded, and a series of hastily typed internal memos from a completely different company. A tightness constricted my chest, a low, simmering pressure building behind my ribs. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms over my chest, my gaze fixed on the ceiling as if searching for an escape route. When Mark arrived, whistling a cheerful tune, I didn't look up immediately. "Everything alright?" he asked, his tone light. I finally met his eyes, and the slight narrowing of my gaze spoke volumes. "Just reviewing some old files," I said, my voice deliberately flat. The silence that followed felt heavy, charged with unspoken accusations.

[story 3]

It started with a misplaced calendar entry. I was proofreading Mark’s shared schedule, a routine task, when I noticed a recurring block labeled 'OmniCorp Strategy Session.' I pulled up the meeting invite, expecting a detailed agenda. Instead, it was a generic placeholder. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I scrolled down, noticing the repeated, almost identical meeting times across several weeks. A cold, hard certainty settled in my stomach. I closed the laptop with a sharp snap that echoed in the quiet apartment. When Mark walked in, radiating easy confidence, I didn't offer a greeting. I simply pointed to the screen, my hand trembling slightly. "Look at this," I managed, the words coming out strained and sharp. His easy demeanor faltered for a split second, replaced by a flicker of something guarded. He tried to laugh it off, but the sound lacked its usual buoyancy.
